subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Lay out the initial research proposal for applying self-supervised learning (SSL) to structured signals. The project aims to develop a new SSL framework for graph-structured data by exploring contrastive learning objectives. A high-level plan is formulated to guide the initial design phase.
reason: This is the first formal event of the project, taking a pre-existing, informal research idea (represented as the initial `research_goals`) and refining it into a concrete set of goals and an initial `research_plan`. This step is necessary to structure all subsequent design and experimentation phases.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Based on the research plan, design the initial methodological scheme for the graph SSL framework. This involves selecting a Graph Neural Network (GNN) architecture as the encoder, defining two graph augmentation strategies (node dropping and edge perturbation), and formalizing the contrastive loss function. The output is a detailed technical specification of the proposed method.
reason: Following the high-level `proposal` in e_01, the next logical step is to translate the abstract `research_plan` into a concrete technical design. This event creates the first version of the `method_scheme`, which is essential before any implementation or pilot experiments can begin.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Implement a prototype of the proposed graph SSL method and run pilot experiments on a small-scale benchmark dataset (e.g., Cora). The goal is to verify the implementation, debug the training pipeline, and get a preliminary signal on whether the contrastive learning approach with the chosen augmentations can learn meaningful node representations.
reason: With a concrete `method_scheme` defined in e_02, the logical next step is to conduct pilot experiments. This allows for an early, low-cost sanity check of the proposed method's feasibility and provides initial `experiment_results` before committing to more resource-intensive main experiments.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Analyze the results from the pilot experiments. The analysis involves evaluating the learned node embeddings on a downstream classification task and inspecting training dynamics. Results show that performance is only slightly better than a random baseline, and the contrastive loss converges to a poor local minimum, suggesting the augmentation strategy is not effective.
reason: After generating preliminary `experiment_results` in e_03, a formal analysis is the critical next step. This interpretation of the results is necessary to determine if the current `method_scheme` is viable or if it requires revision before proceeding to more costly main experiments. The findings from this analysis will guide the next iteration of the project.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Following the poor pilot results, the methodological scheme is revised. The simple node dropping and edge perturbation augmentations are deemed insufficient. A new augmentation strategy is designed, based on creating alternative graph views using graph diffusion to capture multi-scale structural information, aiming to provide more meaningful positive pairs for the contrastive objective.
reason: The analysis in e_04 concluded that the initial augmentation strategy was ineffective, leading to poor performance. Instead of proceeding with a flawed method, the logical next step is to revisit and revise the `method_scheme`. This event directly addresses the identified weakness by designing a more sophisticated augmentation technique, creating an updated `method_scheme` for the next experimental cycle.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Run a new round of pilot experiments using the revised method scheme. The prototype is updated to incorporate the graph diffusion-based augmentation. The experiment is run again on the Cora dataset to directly assess if the new augmentation strategy resolves the performance issues observed in the previous pilot study.
reason: After revising the `method_scheme` in e_05 to address the poor performance identified in e_04, the logical next step is to validate the new design. A second round of `pilot_experiments` is a cost-effective way to test if the new augmentation strategy is more effective before committing resources to full-scale `main_experiments`.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Analyze the results from the second round of pilot experiments. The analysis shows the new graph diffusion-based augmentation leads to a significant performance improvement on the downstream task. The contrastive loss converges properly, and the learned embeddings show better separation in a t-SNE visualization, confirming the new scheme's effectiveness.
reason: Following the second round of pilot experiments in e_06, a detailed analysis is necessary to determine if the revised method from e_05 was successful. This step validates the new approach, confirming it fixes the issues found in e_04, and provides the justification needed to proceed to larger-scale main experiments.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Following the successful pilot study, launch the main experiments. This involves running the validated SSL method on a suite of larger benchmark graph datasets (e.g., Citeseer, Pubmed, OGB datasets). The goal is to rigorously evaluate the method's performance against state-of-the-art baselines and conduct ablation studies to understand the contribution of the new augmentation strategy.
reason: The analysis in e_07 confirmed the effectiveness of the revised method in pilot experiments. The logical progression is to now scale up to main experiments on larger, more diverse datasets to rigorously validate the approach, compare against baselines, and gather the core results needed for the final paper.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Systematically analyze the comprehensive results from the main experiments. This includes creating performance comparison tables against baselines, plotting learning curves, and interpreting the ablation study results to quantify the impact of the graph diffusion augmentation. The findings confirm that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing approaches on most benchmarks.
reason: After generating comprehensive `experiment_results` from the main experiments in e_08, a detailed `analysis` is the critical next step. This allows the project to consolidate its findings, validate the method's superiority against baselines, and confirm that the `research_goals` have been met, paving the way for writing the paper.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Begin drafting the research paper, starting with the methodology section. This involves translating the finalized technical scheme, including the GNN encoder and the graph diffusion-based augmentation, into a clear and formal description suitable for publication.
reason: With the main experiments analyzed and the method's success confirmed in e_09, the project's focus shifts from experimentation to dissemination. Starting to write the paper is the natural next step, and the methodology section is a logical place to begin as the `method_scheme` is now stable and well-understood.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Draft the 'Experiments' and 'Results' sections of the paper. This involves describing the experimental setup, datasets, and evaluation protocol, as well as creating tables and figures to present the main performance comparisons and ablation study results from the main experiments.
reason: With the methodology section drafted (e_10) and the main experimental results fully analyzed (e_09), the logical next step in the writing process is to document these findings. This event translates the structured `experiment_results` into the narrative and visual format of a research paper, forming the core evidence for the paper's claims.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: While reviewing the draft of the results section, it's determined that qualitative analysis is needed to strengthen the paper's narrative. A new analysis is conducted to generate t-SNE visualizations of the learned node embeddings from the main experiments, aiming to visually demonstrate the quality of the learned representations.
reason: After drafting the quantitative results in e_11, a common next step is to add qualitative evidence to make the paper more intuitive and compelling. This analysis event is a direct reaction to the writing process, generating new visualizations from the existing main `experiment_results` to better illustrate the method's effectiveness.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Update the 'Results' section of the paper to include the new qualitative analysis. This involves adding the t-SNE visualizations generated in the previous step and writing text that explains how these plots visually confirm the model's ability to learn discriminative representations, strengthening the paper's overall argument.
reason: The qualitative analysis in e_12 was specifically conducted to enhance the paper. Therefore, the immediate next step is to integrate these new visualizations and their interpretation into the existing `paper_paragraph` draft, making the results section more compelling before moving on to other parts of the paper.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Draft the 'Introduction' and 'Conclusion' sections of the paper. The introduction frames the problem, highlights limitations of prior work, and outlines the paper's contributions. The conclusion summarizes the key findings from the experiments and suggests avenues for future work, such as applying the method to different types of structured data.
reason: With the core 'Methodology' and 'Results' sections drafted and refined (e_10, e_11, e_13), the next logical step in the writing process is to create the narrative frame. Drafting the introduction and conclusion synthesizes the initial `research_goals` and final `experiment_results` into a cohesive story, which is essential for producing a complete first draft of the paper.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: While reviewing the draft paper, a potential reviewer concern is identified: the sensitivity of the model to the hyperparameters of the graph diffusion process. A new set of experiments is run to analyze the model's performance across a range of diffusion parameters. The goal is to demonstrate the method's robustness and provide practical guidance for parameter tuning.
reason: The process of writing the paper (e_14) often reveals gaps in the experimental evaluation. Anticipating reviewer questions about hyperparameter sensitivity is a common and crucial step. This targeted `main_experiments` event is necessary to strengthen the paper's claims of robustness before submission.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Analyze the results from the hyperparameter sensitivity experiments. The analysis involves plotting model performance against the diffusion parameter values. The findings show that the model's performance is stable across a reasonable range of settings, confirming its robustness and providing clear guidance for its practical application.
reason: After running new experiments in e_15 to test hyperparameter sensitivity, the immediate next step is to analyze these results. This analysis is crucial to confirm the method's robustness and to determine how to integrate these findings into the paper to preemptively address potential reviewer critiques.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Incorporate the results of the hyperparameter sensitivity analysis into the paper. A new subsection is added to the appendix or experimental section, including plots and a discussion of the model's robustness. This makes the paper more comprehensive and preemptively addresses potential reviewer questions.
reason: The sensitivity analysis in e_16 was performed specifically to strengthen the paper. Therefore, the immediate next step is to integrate these new `experiment_results` into the `paper_paragraph` draft. This completes the experimental reporting and makes the paper's evaluation more robust ahead of final revisions and submission.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: Conduct a full review and revision of the entire paper draft. This involves polishing the language, improving the narrative flow, ensuring consistency across all sections, and checking figures and tables for clarity. The goal is to produce a submission-ready manuscript based on internal feedback from co-authors.
reason: The paper has been drafted in pieces and augmented with new results (e_17). A holistic revision is now necessary to unify the narrative, polish the writing, and prepare the manuscript for submission. This is a critical final step before the project's primary output is complete.

subject: CS-AI/ML
description: The research project is concluded. The final paper has been internally reviewed, revised, and is now ready for submission to a target conference or journal. All primary research goals have been met and documented.
reason: The project has reached a natural conclusion. The methodology is stable (e_05), the main experiments are comprehensive and successful (e_09, e_16), and the resulting paper has been drafted and internally polished (e_18). At this stage, the core research work is complete, and the next step (submission) marks the end of this research cycle.
